metadata.description: Two plants used in traditional Polynesian medicine, Geophila repens and Centella asiatica were selected for a study of their ethnobotany and ecology. Ethnobotanical data was collected via interviews with healers or other knowledgeable persons on the island of Mo‚Äö√Ñ√¥orea. Assays for bioactivity were performed using Artemia salina for cytotoxicity assays and Saccharomyces cereviae for antifungal assays. Transects of the forest environment in which Geophila repens grows were conducted and those species that were part of its community were documented and analyzed for community associations. A small germination study was performed but no seeds germinated. Cytotoxicity tests showed that an aqueous extract of Centella asiatica significantly enhanced shrimp survivorship; an aqueous extract of Geophila repens showed the next highest survivorship, an ethanol extract of positive control Cantharanthus roseus resulted in the lowest survivorship, followed by the negative control water. The antifungal assay design was inadequate to produce significant results. Ordination showed a distinct community assemblage at one of the three main ecological study sites. This study suggests that both Centella asiatica and Geophila repens possess some biologically active compounds.
metadata.title: The ethnobotany and ecology of Geophila repens and Centella asiatica on Moorea, French Polynesia
